flashcards.back: Merge Sort is a stable, divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that recursively divides the array into halves, sorts each half, and merges them back together. It guarantees O(n log n) time complexity in all cases (worst, average, and best). It requires O(n) auxiliary space for the merging step.

Quick Sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that selects a 'pivot' element and partitions the array around the pivot, placing smaller elements before and larger elements after the pivot. It then recursively sorts the sub-arrays. Average case is O(n log n), but worst case (poor pivot selection) degrades to O(n²).

flashcards.front: Describe the Merge Sort algorithm steps.
flashcards.back:
1. Divide: Find the middle point and divide array into two halves.
2. Recursively sort: Call merge sort on both halves.
3. Merge: Combine the two sorted halves into a single sorted array. Pseudocode: mergeSort(arr, l, r): if l<r: mid=(l+r)/2; mergeSort(arr,l,mid); mergeSort(arr,mid+1,r); merge(arr,l,mid,r).

flashcards.front: Describe the Quick Sort algorithm steps.
flashcards.back:
1. Choose a pivot element (strategies: first, last, random, or median-of-three).
2. Partition: Rearrange array so elements < pivot are on left, elements > pivot are on right.
3. Recursively apply Quick Sort to left and right sub-arrays. Pseudocode: quickSort(arr, low, high): if low<high: pi=partition(arr,low,high); quickSort(arr,low,pi-1); quickSort(arr,pi+1,high).

flashcards.front: Compare the time complexities of Merge Sort and Quick Sort.
flashcards.back: Merge Sort: O(n log n) in all cases (worst, average, best) — predictable performance. Quick Sort: O(n log n) average and best case; O(n²) worst case (when array is already sorted/nearly sorted and pivot is first/last element). Quick Sort is faster in practice due to better cache performance and lower overhead.

flashcards.front: Compare the space complexities of Merge Sort and Quick Sort.
flashcards.back: Merge Sort requires O(n) auxiliary space for the merging step (not in-place). Quick Sort requires O(log n) stack space for recursion in average case, but O(n) in worst case due to unbalanced partitions. Quick Sort is considered in-place because it uses only O(1) extra space apart from recursion stack.

flashcards.front: What are the key differences between Merge Sort and Quick Sort?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort is stable, requires O(n) extra space, and guarantees O(n log n) time. Quick Sort is unstable, in-place (O(1) aux space), but has O(n²) worst case. Merge Sort is better for linked lists (no random access needed) and external sorting; Quick Sort is preferred for arrays due to better cache locality.

flashcards.front: When should you use Merge Sort vs Quick Sort in practical applications?
flashcards.back: Use Merge Sort when: stability required, guaranteed O(n log n) needed, sorting linked lists, or external file sorting. Use Quick Sort when: average performance matters more than worst-case, working with arrays in main memory, and cache efficiency is important. Many standard library sorts (like Java's Arrays.sort for objects) use Quick Sort or its variants.
